option.name: updatedContent
option.value:
# Deployment Setup Checklist
Complete these steps to enable automatic Elastic Beanstalk deployments.
## Phase 1: AWS Account Configuration (One-time Setup)
### GitHub OIDC Provider - [ ] Go to AWS IAM Console - [ ] Navigate to Identity Providers - [ ] Click "Create Provider" - [ ] Provider Type: OpenID Connect - [ ] Provider URL: https://token.actions.githubusercontent.com - [ ] Audience: sts.amazonaws.com - [ ] Copy the provider ARN (format: arn:aws:iam::275928091008:oidc-provider/...)
### Create IAM Role - [ ] Go to AWS IAM Console → Roles - [ ] Create new role named: `github-actions-role` - [ ] Select "Web identity" trust relationship - [ ] Identity provider: token.actions.githubusercontent.com - [ ] Audience: sts.amazonaws.com - [ ] Add condition (trust policy): ``` token.actions.githubusercontent.com:sub like repo:YOUR_ORG/elastic-beanstalk-deploy:* ``` (Replace YOUR_ORG with your GitHub org/username)
### Attach Permissions - [ ] Attach inline policy to `github-actions-role`: ```json { "Version": "2012-10-17", "Statement": [ { "Effect": "Allow", "Action": [ "s3:CreateBucket", "s3:ListBucket", "s3:PutObject", "s3:GetObject" ], "Resource": [ "arn:aws:s3:::elastic-beanstalk-deploy-bucket", "arn:aws:s3:::elastic-beanstalk-deploy-bucket/*" ] }, { "Effect": "Allow", "Action": [ "elasticbeanstalk:CreateApplicationVersion", "elasticbeanstalk:UpdateEnvironment", "elasticbeanstalk:DescribeEnvironments" ], "Resource": "*" } ] } ```
## Phase 2: Elastic Beanstalk Setup
### Create Application - [ ] Go to AWS Elastic Beanstalk Console - [ ] Click "Create Application" - [ ] Application name: `simple-application` - [ ] Application type: Java - [ ] Click "Create"
### Create Environment - [ ] Click "Create Environment" (or "Create new environment") - [ ] Environment name: `simple-application-env` - [ ] Platform: Java (Choose appropriate Java/Corretto version) - [ ] Preset: Single instance (for development) or High availability (for production) - [ ] Click "Create environment" - [ ] Wait for environment to be "Ready" (this may take 5-10 minutes)
### Note Environment Details - [ ] Copy the environment URL (e.g., simple-application-env.elasticbeanstalk.com) - [ ] Note the Endpoint URL
## Phase 3: GitHub Secrets (if needed)
- [ ] Go to your GitHub repository - [ ] Settings → Secrets and variables → Actions - [ ] No GitHub secrets needed! (Using OIDC)
## Phase 4: Verify Files Are in Place
- [ ] `.github/workflows/deploy.yml` exists ✓ - [ ] `simple_application/pom.xml` exists ✓ - [ ] `deploy/Procfile` exists ✓ - [ ] `simple_application/src/main/resources/templates/home.html` exists ✓ - [ ] `simple_application/src/main/java/org/mave/simple_application/HomeController.java` exists ✓
## Phase 5: First Deployment
### Option A: Automatic (Recommended) - [ ] Commit all changes to git - [ ] Push to `main` or `master` branch ```bash git add . git commit -m "Add GitHub Actions deployment workflow" git push origin main ``` - [ ] Go to GitHub → Actions tab - [ ] Watch "Build and Deploy to Elastic Beanstalk" workflow - [ ] Wait for deployment to complete (usually 5-10 minutes)
### Option B: Manual Trigger (Test) - [ ] Go to GitHub repository - [ ] Click "Actions" tab - [ ] Select "Build and Deploy to Elastic Beanstalk" workflow - [ ] Click "Run workflow" - [ ] Select branch: main - [ ] Click "Run workflow"
## Phase 6: Verification
After deployment completes:
- [ ] Go to GitHub Actions → Check workflow status (should be ✓ green) - [ ] Go to AWS Elastic Beanstalk → Environment should be "Ready" - [ ] Get the environment URL from EB Console - [ ] Open the URL in a browser - [ ] Verify you see "Welcome to the Simple Application!" message - [ ] Check application logs if needed
## Phase 7: Configure Auto-Deployments
- [ ] Verify workflow triggers on push to `main` (or `master`) - [ ] Test by making a code change: - [ ] Create a new branch - [ ] Make a small change (e.g., update the greeting in `home.html`) - [ ] Commit and push to `main` - [ ] Workflow should trigger automatically - [ ] Verify deployment completes successfully - [ ] Check the updated content on your EB environment
## Phase 8: Customization (Optional)
- [ ] Review `.github/workflows/deploy.yml` - [ ] Customize environment variables if needed: - [ ] AWS_REGION - [ ] EB_APPLICATION_NAME - [ ] EB_ENVIRONMENT_NAME - [ ] S3_BUCKET_NAME - [ ] Read `DEPLOYMENT_QUICKSTART.md` for quick reference - [ ] Read `DEPLOYMENT_SETUP.md` for detailed information
## Troubleshooting During Setup
### Workflow doesn't trigger - [ ] Verify you pushed to `main` or `master` branch - [ ] Check workflow file is in `.github/workflows/deploy.yml` - [ ] Go to Actions tab to see if it shows up
### "Role not found" error - [ ] Verify IAM role `github-actions-role` exists - [ ] Check trust policy includes your GitHub repo - [ ] Replace `YOUR_ORG` with actual organization/username
### "Access Denied" for S3 - [ ] Verify inline policy is attached to role - [ ] Check S3 bucket name matches in policy and workflow - [ ] Try deleting and recreating the role
### "Elastic Beanstalk app not found" - [ ] Create the application and environment first in EB Console - [ ] Verify names match: `simple-application` and `simple-application-env` - [ ] Wait for environment to be in "Ready" state
### Maven build fails - [ ] Check Java version in workflow (should be 25) - [ ] Check `pom.xml` matches (Java version 25) - [ ] Try running locally: `cd simple_application && ./mvnw clean package`
## Need Help?
1. **Quick Questions**: See `DEPLOYMENT_QUICKSTART.md` 2. **Detailed Setup**: See `DEPLOYMENT_SETUP.md` 3. **Workflow Errors**: Check GitHub Actions logs 4. **Deployment Issues**: Check AWS EB environment events/logs
## Success Indicators
You'll know it's working when: - ✅ GitHub Actions workflow runs without errors - ✅ Code is built and packaged successfully - ✅ Bundle appears in S3 bucket - ✅ New version created in Elastic Beanstalk - ✅ Environment shows "Ready" status - ✅ Application is live at the EB URL - ✅ Every push to `main` triggers automatic deployment
---
**Total Setup Time**: 30-45 minutes **Approval Required**: None (OIDC-based, no credentials stored) **Manual Steps After Setup**: ZERO (Fully automated)

option.name: updatedContent
option.value:
# GitHub Actions Deployment - Quick Start Guide
## What Was Created
✅ **GitHub Actions Workflow** (`.github/workflows/deploy.yml`) - Automatically builds your Spring Boot application with Maven - Packages it into a deployment bundle with your Procfile - Uploads to S3 - Creates a new Elastic Beanstalk application version - Deploys automatically to your EB environment - Verifies deployment status
✅ **Homepage** (`.src/main/resources/templates/home.html`) - Thymeleaf template with greeting message
## Quick Setup (5 Steps)
### Step 1: Set Up AWS OIDC Provider (One-time)
In AWS IAM Console, create an Identity Provider: - **Provider Type**: OpenID Connect - **Provider URL**: `https://token.actions.githubusercontent.com` - **Audience**: `sts.amazonaws.com`
### Step 2: Create IAM Role (One-time)
Create role `github-actions-role` with trust relationship:
```json { "Version": "2012-10-17", "Statement": [ { "Effect": "Allow", "Principal": { "Federated": "arn:aws:iam::275928091008:oidc-provider/token.actions.githubusercontent.com" }, "Action": "sts:AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity", "Condition": { "StringEquals": { "token.actions.githubusercontent.com:aud": "sts.amazonaws.com" }, "StringLike": { "token.actions.githubusercontent.com:sub": "repo:YOUR_ORG/elastic-beanstalk-deploy:*" } } } ] } ```
### Step 3: Attach Permissions Policy (One-time)
Attach this policy to `github-actions-role`:
```json { "Version": "2012-10-17", "Statement": [ { "Effect": "Allow", "Action": ["s3:*"], "Resource": "arn:aws:s3:::elastic-beanstalk-deploy-bucket*" }, { "Effect": "Allow", "Action": [ "elasticbeanstalk:CreateApplicationVersion", "elasticbeanstalk:UpdateEnvironment", "elasticbeanstalk:DescribeEnvironments" ], "Resource": "*" } ] } ```
### Step 4: Create Elastic Beanstalk Resources (One-time)
**Option A: Using AWS CLI** ```bash # Create application aws elasticbeanstalk create-application \ --application-name simple-application \ --region us-east-1
# Create environment aws elasticbeanstalk create-environment \ --application-name simple-application \ --environment-name simple-application-env \ --solution-stack-name "64bit Amazon Linux 2 v5.9.10 running Corretto 21" \ --region us-east-1 ```
**Option B: Using AWS Console** 1. Navigate to Elastic Beanstalk 2. Create Application: `simple-application` 3. Create Environment: `simple-application-env` 4. Select Java platform (Corretto 21+)
### Step 5: Deploy
Simply push to your `main` or `master` branch: ```bash git push origin main ```
The workflow will automatically: 1. Build your application 2. Package it 3. Upload to S3 4. Deploy to Elastic Beanstalk
## How It Works
``` ┌─────────────────┐ │ Code Push │ │ (main/master) │ └────────┬────────┘ │ ▼ ┌─────────────────────────┐ │ GitHub Actions │ │ - Maven Build │ │ - Create ZIP Bundle │ │ - Upload to S3 │ │ - Create EB Version │ │ - Deploy to EB │ │ - Verify Status │ └────────┬────────────────┘ │ ▼ ┌──────────────────────────┐ │ Elastic Beanstalk │ │ Environment Ready! │ │ Application Live ✅ │ └──────────────────────────┘ ```
## Workflow Customization
Edit `.github/workflows/deploy.yml` to customize:
- **AWS Region**: Change `AWS_REGION` (default: us-east-1) - **EB Application**: Change `EB_APPLICATION_NAME` (default: simple-application) - **EB Environment**: Change `EB_ENVIRONMENT_NAME` (default: simple-application-env) - **S3 Bucket**: Change `S3_BUCKET_NAME` (default: elastic-beanstalk-deploy-bucket)
## Monitoring Your Deployment
### GitHub 1. Go to your repository on GitHub 2. Click "Actions" tab 3. View the workflow execution logs
### AWS Console 1. Go to Elastic Beanstalk 2. Select your environment 3. Check "Events" tab for deployment status 4. View application URL in environment details
## Verification
After deployment: 1. Get your EB environment URL from AWS Console 2. Visit the URL to see your homepage with the greeting 3. Verify the application is running correctly
## Troubleshooting
| Issue | Solution | |-------|----------| | "Role not found" | Verify IAM role ARN and OIDC provider setup | | "S3 forbidden" | Check IAM role has S3 permissions | | "EB app not found" | Create application and environment in AWS first | | "Maven build fails" | Check Java version in workflow matches pom.xml | | "Jar not found" | Verify build output path is correct |
## Key Features
✅ **Fully Automated** - No manual steps after initial setup ✅ **Secure** - Uses OIDC, no stored AWS credentials ✅ **Fast** - Maven caching speeds up builds ✅ **Reliable** - Includes deployment verification ✅ **Zero-Downtime** - EB handles rolling updates ✅ **Easy Rollback** - Previous versions available in EB
## Next Steps
1. Complete the 5-step setup above 2. Push code to `main` or `master` branch 3. Watch it deploy automatically 4. Update `DEPLOYMENT_SETUP.md` for detailed info

option.name: updatedContent
option.value:
# Elastic Beanstalk Deployment Setup Guide
This guide explains the setup required for the automated GitHub Actions deployment workflow.
## Prerequisites
### 1. AWS Account Setup
You need to have an AWS account with permissions to: - Create and manage Elastic Beanstalk applications and environments - Create and manage S3 buckets - Assume IAM roles via GitHub's OpenID Connect provider
### 2. GitHub OIDC Provider Configuration
The workflow uses GitHub's OpenID Connect (OIDC) to securely assume the AWS IAM role without storing long-lived credentials.
To set this up:
1. Go to your AWS IAM console 2. Create a new Identity Provider: - Provider type: OpenID Connect - Provider URL: `https://token.actions.githubusercontent.com` - Audience: `sts.amazonaws.com`
3. Create an IAM role for GitHub Actions: ```json { "Version": "2012-10-17", "Statement": [ { "Effect": "Allow", "Principal": { "Federated": "arn:aws:iam::275928091008:oidc-provider/token.actions.githubusercontent.com" }, "Action": "sts:AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity", "Condition": { "StringEquals": { "token.actions.githubusercontent.com:aud": "sts.amazonaws.com" }, "StringLike": { "token.actions.githubusercontent.com:sub": "repo:YOUR_GITHUB_ORG/elastic-beanstalk-deploy:*" } } } ] } ```
Replace `YOUR_GITHUB_ORG` with your actual GitHub organization or username.
### 3. IAM Role Permissions
Attach this policy to the `github-actions-role`:
```json { "Version": "2012-10-17", "Statement": [ { "Effect": "Allow", "Action": [ "s3:CreateBucket", "s3:ListBucket", "s3:PutObject", "s3:GetObject" ], "Resource": [ "arn:aws:s3:::elastic-beanstalk-deploy-bucket", "arn:aws:s3:::elastic-beanstalk-deploy-bucket/*" ] }, { "Effect": "Allow", "Action": [ "elasticbeanstalk:CreateApplicationVersion", "elasticbeanstalk:UpdateEnvironment", "elasticbeanstalk:DescribeEnvironments" ], "Resource": "*" } ] } ```
### 4. Elastic Beanstalk Application Setup
Create an Elastic Beanstalk application and environment:
```bash # Create the application aws elasticbeanstalk create-application \ --application-name simple-application \ --region us-east-1
# Create the environment aws elasticbeanstalk create-environment \ --application-name simple-application \ --environment-name simple-application-env \ --solution-stack-name "64bit Amazon Linux 2 v5.9.10 running Corretto 21" \ --region us-east-1 ```
Or use the AWS Console: 1. Go to Elastic Beanstalk 2. Create an application named `simple-application` 3. Create an environment named `simple-application-env` 4. Choose Java platform with Corretto (Java 21 or later)
### 5. S3 Bucket
The workflow will automatically create the S3 bucket if it doesn't exist. However, you can also pre-create it:
```bash aws s3 mb s3://elastic-beanstalk-deploy-bucket --region us-east-1 ```
## Workflow Configuration
The workflow is defined in `.github/workflows/deploy.yml` and includes:
1. **Trigger**: Automatically runs on push to `main` or `master` branches 2. **Build**: Compiles the Maven project and creates a JAR 3. **Package**: Creates a deployment bundle with the JAR and Procfile 4. **Upload**: Uploads the bundle to S3 5. **Version**: Creates a new Elastic Beanstalk application version 6. **Deploy**: Updates the environment to use the new version 7. **Verify**: Checks deployment status
## Customization
You can customize the following environment variables in the workflow:
- `AWS_REGION`: AWS region (default: us-east-1) - `EB_APPLICATION_NAME`: Elastic Beanstalk application name (default: simple-application) - `EB_ENVIRONMENT_NAME`: Elastic Beanstalk environment name (default: simple-application-env) - `S3_BUCKET_NAME`: S3 bucket for deployment bundles (default: elastic-beanstalk-deploy-bucket) - `AWS_ROLE_ARN`: IAM role to assume (default: arn:aws:iam::275928091008:role/github-actions-role)
Edit the `env` section at the top of `.github/workflows/deploy.yml` to change these values.
## Deployment Process
### Automatic Deployment
Every push to `main` or `master` branch will: 1. Trigger the workflow automatically 2. Build and package the application 3. Upload to S3 4. Deploy to Elastic Beanstalk without manual intervention
### Manual Deployment
If needed, you can manually trigger the workflow from GitHub: 1. Go to your GitHub repository 2. Click on "Actions" 3. Select "Build and Deploy to Elastic Beanstalk" 4. Click "Run workflow"
## Monitoring Deployments
You can monitor deployments in two ways:
### GitHub 1. Go to your repository's "Actions" tab 2. Click on the workflow run to see detailed logs
### AWS Console 1. Go to Elastic Beanstalk 2. Select your environment 3. Check the "Events" tab for deployment status
## Troubleshooting
### Common Issues
**Issue**: "Role not found" error - Verify the IAM role ARN is correct - Check OIDC provider is configured properly for your repository
**Issue**: "S3 bucket access denied" - Verify the IAM role has S3 permissions - Check bucket name in the workflow matches the policy
**Issue**: "Elastic Beanstalk application not found" - Create the application in AWS first - Verify application and environment names match the workflow
**Issue**: "Deployment stuck in updating state" - Check AWS EB environment logs in the console - Ensure the Procfile is correct - Verify the JAR file is building properly
## Security Best Practices
1. **No Credentials in Code**: The workflow uses OIDC for secure credential management 2. **Limited Permissions**: The IAM role has minimal required permissions 3. **Environment Isolation**: Consider creating separate environments for dev/staging/production 4. **Code Review**: Require pull request reviews before merging to `main`
## Next Steps
1. Set up the AWS OIDC provider (if not already configured) 2. Create the IAM role with the required permissions 3. Create the Elastic Beanstalk application and environment 4. Push code to your `main` or `master` branch to trigger the first deployment
